translated: If you want something good, you have to work hard and be patient. Good things do not come easily to those who are lazy.

ref: The rainbow will be the sign of that solemn promise. Noah and His Family
text: Dila Wen yaa weeni Nowa ayen, <<Jabuui ale a sak nɔiyeŋka ate mi nye mi ale fi ale ŋanvuta meena tɛŋka zuk la ale nna.>>

ref: All people on earth are descendants of Noah's three sons.
text: Nowa nidoabilisa batawa jam le la. Bala abe le biak ate nuruma waaŋ tɛŋka meena.

ref: Ham entered the tent and saw him naked, then went back outside and told his brothers.

ref: Shem and Japheth put a robe over their shoulders and walked backwards into the tent. Without looking at their father, they placed it over his body.

translated: When Shem and Japheth heard about what their father had done, they took a cloth and walked backwards to cover their father. They did this so that they would not see their father's nakedness.

ref: This completes the list of Noah's descendants. After the flood their descendants became nations and spread all over the world.
text: Nowa ŋaaŋbisaŋa ale nna. Nyiamu ale jam lik tɛŋka la ŋaaŋnyiŋ ba yaa jam chim ka tɛŋ kpieŋsa a waaŋ tɛŋka meena zuk.

ref: Because of Sarai, the Lord struck the king and everyone in his palace with terrible diseases.

translated: If you take a story and put it in a book, and someone reads the words in the book, they have read the story.

text: Ayen ba siti Iilam naawa Chedorlaomer ale Goyim naawa Tidal ale Sinar naawa Amrapel ale Ellasar naawa Ariok. Nalima ŋanaansi ale ta chaab ale nalima ŋanu.

ref: was full of tar pits, and when the troops from Sodom and Gomorrah started running away, some of them fell into the pits. Others escaped to the hill country.

translated: The people of Viaka were wicked and sinned greatly against God. Sodom and Gomorrah were also wicked, and their people did bad things, so God punished them and destroyed those cities with fire from heaven.

text: Abram le wom ayen wa suoku nidoabinni ba yik ta cheŋ la, wa yaa wi nuruma kobsita ale pi ale naanika ate ba biak ba wa yenni po a sak ba la a nyini ta ba a ga paari Dan.
translated: Abram took his wife and his nephew and all the possessions they had gathered, and the people they had acquired in Haran, and they set out for the land of Canaan, and they arrived there.

translated: He went with his father to the field and I saw that there were three trees and their names were the tree of Baai, the tree of Nuru, and the tree of Mu near the village. The names were Aner, Eskol, and Mamre. These three were near the village.

ref: Four generations later, your descendants will return here and take this land, because only then will the people who live here be so sinful that they deserve to be punished.

translated: The word of the Lord came to Abram, "Do not be afraid. I am your shield and your very great reward." Abram then told his wife Sarai what had happened.

ref: I will give you and them the land in which you are now a foreigner. I will give the whole land of Canaan to your family forever, and I will be their God.

translated: When Keenan went to the farm, he saw a group of children playing under a big tree, and he asked them where they came from and what their names were. He did not see Wen.

translated: Jesus saw two brothers and called them. He said to them, "Come and follow me, and what will I give you?" Jacob answered him, "We will leave everything and follow you because you are the Son of God."

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Alege Iisɔ yaa weeni wa ayen, <<M suowa tanla fi ŋantaŋa, mi mɛ dek a ta ŋanta kama nna yegayega.
translated: If you ask me for advice, I will tell you what I know, but I do not know everything.

ref: “Let's get ready to travel,” Esau said. “I'll go along with you.”
text: Ate Iisɔ yaa weeni ayen, <<Basi ate ti lɔ suik po ate m bani fu ate ti cheŋ.>>
translated: Basi said that if he goes there, he will not return.

ref: Shechem added, “Do this favor for me, and I'll give whatever you want.
text: Ate Sikiim yaa weeni Daina kowa ale wa tuama ayen, <<Ni ŋman te mu, ate ni ale a yaali jaabuuri meena la, m le te ni.
translated: When Sikiim and Daina were sitting together, she said, "If you love me, and if you want to marry me, I will love you."

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Jekob bisaŋa yaa jam kpiema zuk a kɔlim ba ŋantaŋa, ale juijui ate ba taŋ ba toawa la.
translated: Jacob took his goat to the market to sell it, but people said it was not healthy.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Ba jam choa ba piisa ale ba niiga ale ba bonsa ale ba ŋanta ale bo diina po ale peelim la kama.
translated: They went and fetched water and firewood and food and yams and brought them to the house.

ref: After taking everything of value from the houses, they dragged away the wives and children of their victims.
text: Ba jam kɔlim ba paaruŋku ale ba nipooba ale ba bisa meena ale ba yieŋa po ŋanta meena a jam ta cheŋ kama.
translated: They took their children and their wives and their animals and their things and went to a place where they could live in peace.

ref: They answered, “Was it right to let our own sister be treated that way?”
text: Alege ba tulisi wa ayen, <<Ti be basi ate wa nye ti toawa ase waai ale pai wadek a te nidoaba a tuesi ŋanta la ya?>>
translated: If someone gives you money, "Will you use it to buy food or will you save it for something important?"

ref: Jacob said to his family and to everyone else who was traveling with him: Get rid of your foreign gods! Then make yourselves acceptable to worship God and put on clean clothes.
text: Ate Jekob yaa weeni wa yenni demma ale baai meena ale bo ale wa la ayen, <<Ni yuk ni wen chaŋsaŋa ale bo ale ni la a basi, abe ni so nidek, abe ni tagri ni gattaŋa.
translated: If you want to follow this path and do what is good, "You must listen carefully and do what you are told, do not be lazy, and do not quarrel with others."

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: Ate ku ale jam tua wa biamu po la, waai ale jam boro yik wa la yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Kan chali yɔgsummoa, fi ale nya ka nidoabili.>>
translated: If you do something good for someone, and the person does not repay you, just say, "Let it be, for God will reward me."

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Iisɔ jam faari wa pooma nyini ka Keenan nipooma po. Ba jam le Ada, Elon Yit denɔwa lie ale Oyolibama Anaya liewa wa jam ka Zibeon Yivi denɔwa ŋaaŋ.
translated: These are the children born to Keenan. The children are Ada, Elon who was the father of Oyolibama, and Anaya who was the child of Zibeon who was also their father.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Dila nyiŋ Iisɔ, Edom ale la a jam kali moata Seir ale bo guutaŋa la.
translated: The descendants of Esau, Edom, live in the hill country of Seir, but the Moabites settled there.

ref: Zibeon's sons were Aiah and Anah—the same Anah who found an oasis in the desert while taking the donkeys of his father out to pasture.
text: Zibeon nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Aiaya ale Anaya. Anaya wa dɛ ale jam nya buli tuilisaŋa gɔnni po wa le jam a nya wa kowa Zibeon bonsaŋa zuk ate si a dɛ la.
translated: Zibeon was thinking about what to do. He called Anaya. Anaya said that he did not know the Buli word for it but he would ask someone who might know and then tell Zibeon later.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Disan nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Uz ale Aran.
translated: They are preparing food for us. He is called Aran.

ref: Jacob lived in the land of Canaan, where his father Isaac had lived,
text: Jekob jam bo ka tɛŋ kaai ate wa kowa jam boro la, Keenan tɛŋka.
translated: Jacob went to the farm and when he came back, Keenan was not there.

ref: We were out in the field, tying up bundles of wheat. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles gathered around and bowed down to it.”
text: Ti boro a bobi kpari ŋanta laŋka po ate mi kpari ŋantaŋa deri yiti zaani, alege ate nama bobaŋa a zaani gilim mi koori tɛŋ di niŋ.>>
translated: If you want to learn something well and remember it for a long time, you should practice it often and use it in your daily life.

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Wa suataŋa yaa weeni ayen, <<Fi a yaali ayen fi a dɛ naam tama zuk kama? Fi le sum a nya tama zuwa? Ate ba yaa ŋmasi kisi wa yega wa gɔdaasimaŋa nyiŋ ale wa le weeni diila nyiŋ.
translated: When the elder finished speaking, he asked, "Do you all agree with what I have said? Or is there someone who has something different to say? If not, let us all work together so that we can achieve what we have discussed."

ref: his father Jacob said to him, “I want you to go to your brothers. They are with the sheep near Shechem.” “Yes, sir,” Joseph answered.
text: Izirali yaa weeni Josefi ayen, <<Ase fi le poom seba la, fi suataŋa a ta piisaŋa a moata ka Sikiim ate ŋa a dɛ. Jam, mi a yaali ayen m tom fu kama ba jigi.>>
translated: Israel said to Joseph, "Your brothers are pasturing the flock at Shechem. Come, I will send you to them." Joseph answered, "Here I am."

ref: Joseph answered, “I'm looking for my brothers who are watching the sheep. Can you tell me where they are?”
text: Wa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Mi a gisi ka m suataŋa. Fi baga a dak ba le ta ba piisaŋa a bo juijui ate ŋa a dɛ la?>>
translated: He/she said to me, "I am going to the market. Can you help me carry these things or do you want to stay at home?"

ref: When the Midianite merchants came by, Joseph's brothers took him out of the well, and for
text: Midian geruŋdɔma ale jam paari la, wa suataŋa yaa dari Josefi yeeri vinni po a pai wa da kpana ligbie pisiye a te Ismael demma ate ba ta wa cheŋ.
translated: Midian traders passed by, and they pulled Joseph up out of the cistern and sold him for twenty pieces of silver to the Ishmaelites, who took him to Egypt.

ref: All of Jacob's children came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said, “I will go to my grave, mourning for my son.” So Jacob kept on grieving.
text: Wa nidoabilisaŋa ale wa liema a jam ayen ba yik wa ate wa sui yɔk, alege wa zɛri. Wa weeni ayen, <<Aaya, mi a kum kama ayen m ga siŋ vɔrup a cheŋ m biika jigi.>> Dila wa kowa a yaa kum wa biika.
translated: They gathered together and discussed what they should do so that they would not suffer, and then they agreed. One of them said, "No, I will not eat until I see the person who took my goat." Then they all decided not to eat.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Alege dila meena, Midian demma a pai Josefi a da ka Ejipiti a te Potifar, Faaro niŋdeerɔma wanyi, limisidɔma kpagni.
translated: When they arrived there, the Midianites sold Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar, one of Pharaoh’s officials, the captain of the guard.

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Va ŋala po, Juda yaa basi wa suataŋa ale cheŋ a ga bo le nuru ate wa nyini Adullam tɛŋka. Wa yuei jam le Yira.
translated: At that time, Judah left his brothers and went down to stay with a man of Adullam named Hirah. There Judah met the daughter of a Canaanite man named Shua.

ref: But Er was very evil, and the Lord took his life.

translated: The man called Juda went to the town to look for food but did not find any, so he returned home.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.

translated: Then he left from there and escaped to the cave of Adullam; when his brothers and all his father’s house heard it, they went down there to him.

ref: As Tamar was being dragged off, she sent someone to tell her father-in-law, “The man who gave me this ring, this cord, and this walking stick is the one who got me pregnant.”
text: Ase ba le ta wa a nyini la, wa yaa tom tomtomiŋ a weeni wa ŋaandɔwa ayen, <<Mi ta ka puuk ale nuru waai ale soa ŋantaŋa de la. Nya ase fi a nin baga a miŋ ase ka wana nifelinni ale miik ale wa tachek ale nna.>>
translated: When they arrived at the house, they saw the old woman sitting with her grandchildren, and she said, "I have told you many times that you should not play near the river. If you do not listen to me, something bad might happen to you."

ref: “Those are mine!” Judah admitted. “She's a better person than I am, because I broke my promise to let her marry my son Shelah.” After this, Judah never slept with her again.
text: Juda yaa miŋ ŋantaŋa ale weeni ayen, <<Wa welema a gaam mi kama, ase m le kan pai wa a te m biika Sɛla la.>> Wa kan ŋman a goa ale wa.
translated: Then Judas went to the chief priests and said, "What will you give me if I hand him over to you?" So they counted out thirty silver coins for him.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.
text: Wa kpagni ale nya ayen Naawen a bo ale wa la, ate Naawen a te wa niaka wie meena po la,
translated: If you do good, God sees it and will bless you, but if you do bad, God will also see and punish you.

ref: Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's coat until her husband came home.
text: Wa yaa jam pai garukku a dueni ka wa tɛŋ ate wa kpagni a ga jam yeri.
translated: He/she went and took the money from the house and then came back and gave the money to his/her mother.

ref: While Joseph was in prison, both the king's personal servant and his chief cook made the king angry. So he had them thrown into the same prison with Joseph.
text: ate wa yaa pai ba a nyo ligrɔma kpagni nisima po, juijui dek ate ba jam lik Josefi la.
translated: When they had finished everything required by the law of the Lord, they returned to Galilee, to their own town of Nazareth.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ligrɔma kpagni a jam pai ba a nyo ka Josefi nisa po ate wa a nya ba zuk. Ba ale jam bo likka dɔkku po ate ku beni magadek la,
translated: The women went to the house early in the morning and saw that Joseph was not there. They were surprised and wondered what could have happened to him.

ref: The next morning, when Joseph went to see the men, he could tell they were upset,
text: Vari ale veenti ate Josefi a jam ba jigi la, wa yaa jam nya ale ba sue a kaasi.
translated: When they arrived where Joseph was staying, they greeted him and he welcomed them into the house.

ref: The king's personal servant told Joseph, “In my dream I saw a vine
text: Ate daam teerɔ kpagni yaa weeni Josefi wa gɔdaasimaŋa. Wa weeni wa ayen, <<M gɔdaasimaŋa po m nya ka tiib m niŋ,
translated: When they heard this, they called Joseph the dreamer. They said, "The dreamer is coming here to see us."

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Alege ku dan cheŋ nalimnyiini ale fu, teeri ate fi sak nyemagsika ale mu. Weeni m wari a te Faaro ate wa yeeri mu a ta nyini likka dɔkku de po a nyini.
translated: When you go to the farm in the morning, you must take your hoe and machete with you. If you forget them at home, you will have to return and get them before you can start working.

ref: I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here in Egypt I haven't done anything to deserve being thrown in jail.
text: Diila ba fɛ mi kama a ta nyini Yiburu demma tɛŋka a nyini, ate dela mɛ mi an nye wari ya a magsi ba a nyo mu likka dɔk po.>>
translated: If you want to go to Yiburu, you should go early in the morning, because if you delay, you may not find a car to take you there.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: alege wa jam pai boroborok seerɔ kpagni a yik ka tiib nyiŋ, ase Josefi ale weeni gɔdaasimaŋa kiri diila.
translated: When his father died and was buried, Joseph and his brothers returned to Egypt.

ref: Then seven ugly, skinny cows came up out of the river and
text: Ŋala ŋaaŋnyiŋ niiga ŋayopɔi a buk a lɔrima a nyini Nile benni po a jueli za naa ŋaai ale za beni nɔi la gauŋ.
translated: The first people who settled along the Nile used the river’s water to grow crops and keep animals.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Ku saluiku wa popola yaa ta wa a daani, ate wa tom wi sampoporisaŋa ale yam nyama meena Ejipiti tɛŋka po. Faaro yaa weeni ba wa gɔdaasimaŋa alege waai a karo a weeni ŋa kiri a sak wa.
translated: The ancestors of our people lived in a foreign land, but God brought them out of Egypt. Pharaoh tried to stop them, but God showed his power and set them free.

ref: A young Hebrew, who was a servant of the captain of the guard, was there with us at the time. When we told him our dreams, he explained what each of them meant,
text: Ate Yiburu bi paalik a yaa jam bo dula ale ti, limisidɔma kpagni tomteerɔ. Ti weeni wa ti gɔdaasimaŋa ate wa weeni ŋa kiri a sak ti.
translated: When Yiburu was walking on the road one day, he saw a strange animal. He called his friends so they could also come and see what he had found.

ref: and everything happened just as he said it would. I got my job back, and the cook was put to death.
text: Ate wie a sum tulim nye a magsi chaab ale wa ale weeni ti diila. Mi ŋman pilim nya m kpaŋtaŋa kama, ate nuru nwala ba pai wa a yik tiib nyiŋ.>>
translated: He went to the farm early in the morning and worked there until the sun set. I was tired when I returned home, but my mother gave me food and I felt happy again.

ref: “Your Majesty,” Joseph answered, “I can't do it myself, but God can give a good meaning to your dreams.”
text: Josefi yaa tulisi Faaro ayen, <<Mi an baga a nye, alege Wen ale te Faaro weeni ŋaai ate wa a yaali la.>>
translated: Joseph said to Pharaoh, "It is not I; God will give Pharaoh the answer he desires."

ref: But you couldn't tell it, because these skinny cows were just as skinny as they were before. At once, I woke up.
text: Alege ŋa le poom a nɛ ŋa a nueri la, waai an baga a weeni ayen ŋa nɛ jaaboa. Ŋa lɔrima ase ka ŋa le poom ka diila. Ate mi yaa yiti.
translated: If you have a problem and you tell your friend, maybe the friend will help you. It is good to talk about your problems. That is what I know.

ref: Then seven other heads of grain came up. They were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Ŋala ŋaaŋnyiŋ chɔlombena ŋayopɔi yaa pusi ŋala nyiŋ a kɔ a jeta ate kori vioku a fuk ŋa a kaasi.
translated: If someone wants to eat meat, he should go and buy it from the market or from someone who sells meat.

ref: Joseph replied: Your Majesty, both of your dreams mean the same thing, and in them God has shown what he is going to do.
text: Ate Josefi yaa weeni Faaro ayen, <<Gɔdaasimaŋa meena ka bunyi. Wen a pai sak Faaro wa le za wa nye diila kama.
translated: Joseph said to Pharaoh, "God has shown me what will happen. These are the things Pharaoh saw in his dream."

ref: For seven years Egypt will have more than enough grain,
text: Bena ŋayopɔi ŋandiinta yegayega ale jam Ejipiti tɛŋka po,
translated: When the king died, another king who did not know Joseph ruled over Egypt.
